narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 40% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction is based on your academic profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20—which is right around the average for past students—your LSAT score of 32, which is notably below the typical 36.7, and your school decile rankings of 4th in Year 1 and 3rd in Year 3, both lower than the averages of about 5.7 and 5.5. Your middle family income bracket aligns closely with the dataset norm, you're a full-time student like most applicants, and as a white female, your demographics match common patterns in the data. When the model weighed these details, your Year 3 decile ranking had the strongest pull toward predicting failure, likely because it signals a dip in performance compared to peers. Right behind that was your LSAT score, which also nudged the prediction toward higher risk since it's well below average. Your Year 1 decile followed closely, reinforcing concerns about early academic trends, with family income, undergraduate GPA, racial background, and gender each playing smaller but still failure-leaning roles in sequence. The one factor that slightly favored success was your choice to study full-time, which typically supports better outcomes. Overall, these elements combined to tip the scales against you, even though no single issue was overwhelming. Looking ahead, you could realistically boost your bar exam odds by targeting LSAT-like analytical skills through dedicated practice, as that's a changeable strength for many. Strengthening late-stage study habits—similar to improving those Year 3 rankings—might help via bar prep courses focused on consistency. While past grades can't shift, excelling in any remaining academics or targeted tutoring could reshape future predictions. We encourage reapplying after building these areas, as even small gains often sway models like this one. Hang in there—many students turn predictions around with focused effort.
